common_name: Pineapple Tomato (Giant Bicolor Yellow-Red Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Pineapple produces very large fruits that take significant time to develop — start seeds early to maximize the harvest window before summer heat causes fruit quality to decline.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Pineapple is one of the most visually spectacular heirloom beefsteak tomatoes — producing enormous (1–2 lb), irregular, slightly ribbed fruits with a stunning bicolor pattern of golden-yellow base skin marked with bold red and orange streaking and marbling that creates a stained-glass appearance when sliced. The cut surface reveals a similarly bicolor red-and-yellow flesh pattern — an ornamental quality that makes Pineapple outstanding for fresh salads and sliced presentations where the visual effect can be appreciated. The flavor matches the visual quality: intensely sweet, fruity, and complex with a low-acid, tropical quality that justifies the variety's evocative name — the combination of golden-yellow color and sweet, fruity flavor genuinely suggests tropical fruit. Large, vigorous indeterminate plants require sturdy support (minimum 6-foot cage or stake). As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds can be saved from the most characteristic fruits for replanting. In zone 9, Pineapple produces best in the spring season (March–June) before summer heat arrives; the very large fruits require a relatively long, cool growing period for maximum development.

common_name: Striped German Tomato (Bicolor Yellow-Red Heirloom Beefsteak)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Striped German produces very large fruits on vigorous indeterminate vines — provide sturdy support to 6+ feet and prune to one or two main stems for largest fruit development.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Striped German is a classic bicolor beefsteak heirloom — a large (10–16 oz), irregularly shaped, old-fashioned beefsteak type with golden-yellow skin striped and mottled with red, creating a vibrant, contrasting bicolor appearance on the exterior. The cut interior reveals the characteristic bicolor flesh of this variety: red and yellow sectors arranged in alternating patterns that create an artistic cross-section pattern — a visually striking effect that makes Striped German outstanding for fresh sliced presentations. The flavor is exceptional — sweet, rich, low-acid, and complex in the tradition of old German beefsteak heirlooms. Striped German is believed to have originated in Appalachia from German immigrant seed stock and has been maintained by Appalachian seed savers for generations before entering the commercial heirloom seed trade. As with most large bicolor heirlooms, Striped German produces best in the spring season in zone 9 (March–June) and can be challenging to grow in the high summer heat that damages large fruit quality. The combination of visual beauty, excellent flavor, and historical significance makes Striped German one of the most rewarding heirlooms for zone 9 spring production.

common_name: Japanese Black Trifele Tomato (Dark Pear-Shaped Rich Heirloom Tomato)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Japanese Black Trifele was actually developed in Russia (despite the 'Japanese' name) and introduced to American seed savers through Russian seed trading networks.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Japanese Black Trifele (also called Japanese Truffle) is a Russian heirloom producing unusual pear-shaped to truffle-shaped (the 'trifele' name references the truffle mushroom shape) fruits in a striking deep mahogany-black to brownish-red color with complex, rich, wine-like flavor that has made it one of the most celebrated dark-fleshed heirloom tomatoes in American seed-saving circles. The pear shape and dark color are both distinctive — the elongated, slightly rounded-bottom pear form with its near-black exterior creates an unusual appearance that is immediately recognizable at the farmers market and in the garden. The red-brown flesh has a rich, dense, complex flavor with deep savory undertones that differ from the fruity brightness of standard red or the simple sweetness of yellow tomatoes — a flavor profile well-suited to cooking, sauces, and combinations with cheese and herbs. Despite the 'Japanese' in the name, this variety was developed and preserved in Russia. The medium-sized fruits (4–6 oz) are more practical for everyday use than giant beefsteaks while still providing exceptional flavor. The dark color comes from anthocyanin production in the skin triggered by sun exposure.

common_name: Yellow Brandywine Tomato (Large Sweet Low-Acid Yellow Heirloom)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Yellow Brandywine has a long growing season — start seeds early (6–8 weeks before last frost) to ensure adequate development time before summer heat impacts fruit quality in zone 9.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Yellow Brandywine is the golden-yellow version of the famous Brandywine tomato family — an Amish heirloom with the same potato-leaf foliage characteristic and large, irregular, beefsteak-form fruits as the original pink Brandywine, but producing luminous golden-yellow to apricot-colored fruits with characteristically low acidity and exceptional fruity sweetness. The flavor is distinctly peachy and sweet — lower acid than the pink Brandywine and with a richer, more tropical character that makes Yellow Brandywine exceptional for fresh eating where the clean, sweet, low-acid quality can be fully appreciated. The large, somewhat irregular, golden-yellow fruits have the thin skin typical of heirloom beefsteaks and require careful handling to avoid cracking and bruising. The yellow exterior and orange-yellow interior flesh create an unusual visual quality in sliced fresh presentations — the golden slices pair beautifully with the red and purple tones of other heirloom tomatoes in mixed tomato salads. As an open-pollinated heirloom, seeds can be saved true-to-type. In zone 9, Yellow Brandywine produces best in the spring growing window (March–June) and requires the full spring season to produce its largest and finest fruits.

common_name: Aunt Ruby's German Green Tomato (Large Sweet Green-When-Ripe Heirloom)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.8) soil in full sun; Aunt Ruby's German Green stays green when ripe — tell ripeness by slight softening to pressure, subtle yellowing of the shoulder, and an internal color check revealing amber-green flesh rather than pure green.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Aunt Ruby's German Green is one of the most famous and beloved green-when-ripe heirloom tomatoes — a large (10–16 oz), irregularly shaped beefsteak type that retains a bright, chartreuse-green to apple-green exterior color even at full ripeness, producing green flesh with amber-yellow internal marbling that makes it unique among ripe tomatoes. The flavor is exceptional: sweet, rich, spicy, and complex with a distinctive quality often described as 'herbal' or 'spicy-sweet' — quite different from the fruity sweetness of yellow types or the savory depth of purple/dark varieties. This variety is named for Ruby Arnold of Greeneville, Tennessee, who saved the seed from German origins and preserved it for decades. The green color at full ripeness challenges harvest timing: rather than using color change as a ripeness indicator, judge by gentle pressure (ripe fruits yield slightly, like a ripe peach), by the subtle yellowing of the blossom-end shoulder, and by cutting a sample fruit to check for amber-green flesh and developed seeds. Aunt Ruby's German Green is outstanding in fresh preparations where the unusual green color and spicy-sweet flavor create memorable flavor combinations with standard red and yellow tomatoes.
